title: CEO Interview with Dr. Il Park of Primemas - Semiwiki
gist: Primemas, a fabless startup founded January 2023, claims a switchless CXL memory-expansion architecture (Hublet) that has broken the data-center DRAM wall, delivering pooled coherent memory from add-in cards to petabyte JBOM scale with over a year of in-field sample silicon, aiming for first commercial products in Q3 2026.

insights.claim: Primemas argues the memory footprint of the average data-center workload increasingly exceeds host DRAM capacity, historically forcing customers to over-provision entire servers just to gain memory, wasting cost and energy without adding compute.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: Frames memory-capacity decoupling from compute as the core inefficiency in AI/data-center infrastructure, the demand driver for CXL expansion.
insights.quote: the only fix has been over-provisioning servers just to gain memory, driving up cost and energy use without adding compute value

insights.claim: Primemas targets an 'explosive trifecta' of DRAM limitations, cost, capacity and performance, claiming customers can now expand memory independent of compute without the usual performance penalty of memory-expansion architectures.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: If validated, compute-independent memory scaling improves TCO and energy, threatening the over-provisioning status quo that benefits server and DRAM vendors.
insights.quote: Customers can now expand memory capacity independent of compute, without the performance penalty typical of memory expansion architectures.

insights.claim: Primemas contends the widespread view of CXL as inefficient, immature, high-latency and unproven at scale is outdated, claiming its full PCIe-CXL implementation delivers massive performance gains and meaningful TCO reduction instead.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: A direct bet against CXL skepticism; if real-world data backs it, CXL memory pooling moves from theoretical spec to deployable, reshaping data-center memory economics.
insights.quote: Much of the market still views CXL as inefficient or immature, assuming added latency and that it is unproven at scale. That perception is outdated
insights.locator: What application areas are your strongest?

insights.claim: Primemas says it turned pooled, shared, hardware-based coherent memory from a theoretical PCIe-CXL spec into working reality spanning add-in cards, 1U systems and JBOM configurations in both switchless and switch architectures.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: Breadth across form factors and scales without re-architecting is the differentiation claim to verify against competitors' switched-fabric offerings.
insights.quote: turning pooled, shared, hardware-based coherent memory from a theoretical spec into a working reality
insights.locator: What application areas are your strongest?

insights.claim: Primemas differentiates with its Hublet (Hub Chiplet) architecture delivering low-latency switchless capacity supporting 14, 16, 32 DIMMs and beyond, avoiding the added latency, cost and power overhead of a switching layer.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: Switchless vs switched-fabric is the key architectural fork in CXL expansion; Primemas bets switchless wins on latency/cost/power.
insights.quote: delivering unparalleled low-latency, switchless capacity that supports 14, 16, 32 DIMMs and beyond
insights.locator: What does the competitive landscape look like

insights.claim: Primemas' Hublet modular-silicon approach offers both pre-defined and quick-turn custom silicon, aiming to eliminate the long lead times typically associated with custom silicon.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: Modular/quick-turn custom silicon is the same time-to-market wedge appearing across chiplet startups.
insights.quote: eliminating the long lead times typically associated with custom silicon

insights.claim: For larger scale Primemas offers JBOM solutions extending its coherent hardware-based memory approach into the petabyte range.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: Petabyte-scale pooled memory targets the largest AI/hyperscale deployments, the highest-value tier of the memory-disaggregation market.
insights.quote: our JBOM solutions extend that same coherent, hardware-based approach into the petabyte range

insights.claim: Primemas says engineering-sample silicon has been in the field gathering real workload and customer feedback for over a year, with first products for purchase planned in Q3 2026.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: Over a year of in-field silicon plus a near-term commercial date sets Primemas ahead of paper-architecture rivals; Q3 2026 is the milestone to watch.
insights.quote: engineering sample silicon has been in the field gathering real workload and customer feedback for over a year
insights.locator: We’re also ahead on real-world validation

insights.claim: Primemas frames DRAM pricing and lead-time volatility as a constant customer pressure point, noting customers architected around a fixed memory-per-server ratio have little flexibility to absorb that volatility.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: Memory-price volatility and disaggregation's portability benefit are the sales hooks; watch DRAM spot pricing as a demand catalyst for CXL expansion.
insights.quote: customers architected around a fixed memory-per-server ratio have little flexibility to absorb that volatility
insights.locator: What keeps your customers up at night?

insights.claim: Primemas positions disaggregated memory as letting operators execute significantly more AI and data services within the same GPU/CPU hardware footprint, driving down opex while handling future workloads.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: Positions memory pooling as an infrastructure-efficiency multiplier for AI clusters, aligning the pitch with hyperscaler capex discipline.
insights.quote: execute significantly more AI and data services within the same hardware footprint

insights.claim: Primemas is developing near-memory compute with both CPU and FPGA options to bring processing closer to data rather than shuttling it across the memory fabric, plus expanded custom RAS features for pooled deployments.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: Near-memory compute plus RAS signals where CXL pooling evolves next; pooling across more hosts raises the reliability bar as a design constraint.
insights.quote: A major focus is near-memory compute, with both CPU and FPGA options.
insights.locator: What new features/technology are you working on?

insights.claim: Primemas CEO Dr. Il Park previously led memory solutions and CAE as VP at SK hynix, headed SoC architecture at Samsung Electronics, and was lead architect for server CPUs at IBM T.J. Watson Research.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: Memory-and-server pedigree from SK hynix, Samsung and IBM is the credibility underwriting for a memory-disaggregation startup.
insights.quote: he was Vice President at SK hynix where he led memory solutions, design innovation, and CAE

insights.claim: Primemas runs a cross-Pacific structure headquartered in Silicon Valley with an R&D center near Pangyo Techno Valley in South Korea to tap silicon-design talent on both sides.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: US-Korea talent structure reflects where memory-adjacent silicon expertise concentrates.
insights.quote: with an R&D center near Pangyo Techno Valley in South Korea
